title: Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome
body_text: Patellofemoral pain syndrome (PFPS) is a broad term used to describe pain in the front of the knee and around the patella. It is sometimes called runner's knee or jumper's knee. PFPS is a common complaint of physically active people. The pain is generally felt in the peripatellar region. Symptoms typically worsen with activities that load the patellofemoral joint in a flexed position, such as running, squatting, and stair climbing. Conservative management includes activity modification, quadriceps strengthening, hip strengthening, patellar taping, and foot orthoses. VMO activation exercises are considered important. Deep squats beyond 90 degrees and the leg extension machine should be avoided in the acute phase. Recommended exercises include: quad sets, straight leg raise, mini squat (0-45 degrees), terminal knee extension, step-up. Hip abductor and external rotator strengthening has been shown to reduce patellofemoral pain.
avoid_list_raw: deep squats, leg extension machine, running (acute), stairs, plyometrics

title: ACL Injury and Reconstruction
body_text: The anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) is one of the major knee ligaments. ACL tears typically occur during sports that involve sudden stops, jumping, or changes in direction. Post-surgical rehabilitation follows a staged protocol. Phase 1 (0-2 weeks): protect the graft, reduce swelling, restore range of motion. Exercises: quad sets, straight leg raise, ankle pumps. Phase 2 (2-6 weeks): progressive weight bearing, stationary cycling, leg press (0-60 degrees). Phase 3 (6-12 weeks): strengthening, proprioception, return to jogging. Phase 4 (12+ weeks): sport-specific training, hop testing. Open-chain knee extension 0-60 degrees is contraindicated in early phases to protect the graft. Return to sport should not occur before 9-12 months. Single-leg squat symmetry index should be >90% before return.
avoid_list_raw: open chain knee extension 0-60 degrees, pivoting, cutting, jumping (early), contact sport

title: Rotator Cuff Tear / Injury
body_text: The rotator cuff consists of four muscles: supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, and subscapularis. Partial and full-thickness tears can occur from acute trauma or degenerative changes. Conservative management is recommended for partial tears and small full-thickness tears. Avoid overhead pressing, upright rows, behind-neck exercises, and wide-grip bench press. Rehabilitation focuses on rotator cuff strengthening in pain-free range, scapular stability, and posterior capsule stretching. Isometric rotator cuff exercises should be used in the acute phase. External rotation strengthening (side-lying ER, band ER) is the cornerstone of conservative management. Suggested supplements: fish oil 3g, collagen 10g with Vitamin C.
avoid_list_raw: overhead press, upright row, behind-neck press, wide-grip bench, kipping pull-ups

title: Achilles Tendinopathy
body_text: Achilles tendinopathy refers to a clinical syndrome characterised by pain, swelling and functional limitation of the Achilles tendon. It can be classified as mid-portion or insertional. The Alfredson eccentric heel drop protocol is the most well-researched conservative treatment. Protocol: 3 sets of 15 repetitions twice daily on a step, with both straight knee and bent knee variations. Isometric loading (5 x 45 seconds at 70% MVC) is also evidence-based. Avoid running, plyometrics, and aggressive calf stretching in the acute phase. Thompson test should be performed to rule out complete rupture. Collagen supplementation (15g with Vitamin C 30 min before exercise) may support tendon healing.
avoid_list_raw: running, plyometrics, jumping, aggressive calf stretch (acute), barefoot running

title: Lumbar Disc Herniation
body_text: A lumbar disc herniation occurs when the nucleus pulposus herniates through the annulus fibrosus. Common levels: L4-L5 and L5-S1. Symptoms include local back pain with or without radiculopathy. McKenzie extension exercises (prone press-ups) are first-line conservative treatment for posterolateral herniations. Avoid all lumbar flexion exercises including crunches, sit-ups, bent-over rows, and heavy deadlifts. Spinal flexion exercises are contraindicated in the acute phase. Core stabilisation (bird dog, dead bug) should be prioritised over spinal flexion. Red flags: saddle anaesthesia, bilateral leg weakness, bladder/bowel dysfunction - require immediate surgical consultation.
avoid_list_raw: sit-ups, crunches, bent-over row, deadlift (acute), good morning, leg press heavy, seated leg curl

recommendation_text: For individuals with acute lateral ankle sprain, clinicians should use the Ottawa Ankle Rules to determine if radiographs are required. RICE protocol for first 48 hours. Supervised exercise therapy including proprioceptive training is recommended over passive treatment alone. Early weight-bearing is encouraged when pain allows. Manual therapy to the ankle and subtalar joint may reduce pain and improve function.
avoid_exercises_raw: running, jumping, agility drills, heavy loaded calf raise (acute)
phase_1: RICE 48h, ankle alphabet, towel scrunches, gentle dorsiflexion
phase_2: Single-leg balance, BOSU, calf raise (bilateral), partial jogging
phase_3: Lateral band walk, agility ladder, return to sport (4-8 weeks)
supplement_note: Bromelain for swelling, Vitamin C for healing

recommendation_text: The Askling H-protocol and the Nordic hamstring exercise are both supported by strong evidence for hamstring injury rehabilitation and prevention. Eccentric loading is the cornerstone of hamstring rehabilitation. Early mobilisation is preferred over immobilisation for Grade I-II strains. Return to sport should be based on strength symmetry (>90% LSI), pain-free sprint, and positive clinical assessment rather than time alone.
avoid_exercises_raw: sprinting (early), Nordic curl (early), running, yoga forward folds, stiff-leg deadlift (early phase)
phase_1: Isometric hamstring contraction, prone lying, pool walking
phase_2: Nordic hamstring eccentric (assisted), stability ball curl, Romanian deadlift (bodyweight)
phase_3: Jogging, sprint progression, full Nordic, return to sport 6-12 weeks
supplement_note: Protein 2.2g/kg/day, Omega-3 3g, Creatine 5g for muscle preservation

recommendation_text: Combined hip and knee exercise is recommended over knee exercise alone for patellofemoral pain. Patellar taping and foot orthoses may be used as adjuncts. Activity modification is essential. Clinicians should prescribe exercises targeting VMO activation, hip abductor strengthening, and external rotator strengthening. Deep squatting and high-impact activities should be avoided until pain-free.
avoid_exercises_raw: deep squats >90 degrees, leg extension machine, running (downhill), plyometrics, stair climbing
phase_1: Quad sets, SLR, mini squat 0-45, non-weight-bearing cycling
phase_2: Step-up, wall squat, hip strengthening, terminal knee extension
phase_3: Wall squat, Bulgarian split squat, jogging on flat surface
source_citation: Witvrouw et al. BJSM 2014; Collins et al. BJSM 2018

document: ACSM Position Stand: Exercise and Overtraining
summary: Overtraining syndrome (OTS) results from an imbalance between training load and recovery. Hallmarks include performance decline, persistent fatigue, mood disturbance, and hormonal dysregulation. Treatment requires complete reduction of training load, with gradual return based on HRV monitoring, mood scales, and resting heart rate. Sleep quality optimisation is essential. The ACSM recommends a minimum 2-4 week recovery period with active monitoring before return to structured training. Ashwagandha and magnesium supplementation have shown benefits in cortisol regulation.
training_restrictions: No high-intensity training, no competition, no heavy lifting
return_criteria: Resting HR normalised, HRV improved, mood scales normalised, subjective energy restored
supplement_recommendations: Ashwagandha 600mg, Magnesium glycinate 400mg, Zinc 25mg, Vitamin D3, Protein 2g/kg
source_citation: ACSM Position Stand 2012; Kreher & Schwartz Sports Health 2012

document: ACSM Guidelines: Stress Fractures in Athletes
summary: Tibial stress fractures are common in runners and military recruits. Management requires immediate non-weight-bearing for 4-6 weeks. Pool running and upper body training are permitted. Bone stress response on MRI indicates risk of progression to complete fracture. Calcium and Vitamin D optimisation is essential. Return to running should follow a graded protocol with imaging confirmation. Female athletes should be evaluated for the Female Athlete Triad / RED-S.
training_restrictions: No running, no jumping, no high impact activity. Crutches if painful walking.
return_criteria: Pain-free walking, imaging clearance, 10% mileage increase rule on return
supplement_recommendations: Calcium 1500mg/day, Vitamin D3 4000 IU, Vitamin K2 100mcg, Magnesium 400mg
source_citation: Warden et al. BJSM 2014; ACSM Bone Stress Injuries Position Stand

mechanism: Repetitive overhead movements, subacromial space narrowing
medical_imaging: MRI for ruling out partial/full RC tear; Ultrasound for dynamic assessment

mechanism: Repetitive wrist extension, gripping activities, overuse of ECRB
symptoms: Pain with wrist extension against resistance
red_flags: Persistent pain >3 months → PRP or shockwave
medical_imaging: Ultrasound to assess tendon thickness and partial tearing

mechanism: Repetitive microtrauma to plantar fascia, insufficient recovery
severity_classification: Mild/Moderate/Severe based on VAS and functional impairment
medical_imaging: Ultrasound to measure fascia thickness (>4mm pathological)
references: Journal of Foot and Ankle Research 2022

mechanism: Sudden acceleration/deceleration, overstretching during kicking or sprinting
severity_classification: Grade I (mild) to Grade III (complete tear)
medical_imaging: MRI for suspected Grade III or labral involvement

mechanism: Sudden change of direction, wide-stance loading, sprinting
medical_imaging: Ultrasound/MRI for Grade III or osteitis pubis

mechanism: Degenerative disc disease, poor posture, repetitive strain
severity_classification: Mild/Moderate/Severe based on NDI score and neurological status
medical_imaging: MRI cervical spine for radiculopathy; X-ray for structural assessment
